semantic_journey: Arabic قصر (qasr) = palace, castle, court; also: to shorten/restrict. The original reasoning acknowledges that Arabic قصر is itself derived from Latin castrum via Aramaic — it is a Latin loanword into Arabic. Greek Καίσαρι = Caesar (dative of Καῖσαρ). Both Arabic qasr and Greek Caesar ultimately derive from Latin (castrum and Caesar are different Latin words, but both are Latin-origin). This is not an Arabic-Greek cognate — both words come from Latin. Shared loanword from Latin.
key_insight: Arabic qasr (palace) derives from Latin castrum; Greek Kaisar is Latin Caesar — both are Latin-origin terms, not an Arabic-Greek cognate.

semantic_journey: Arabic نبت (nabata) = to grow, sprout, germinate. Greek Ναβαταῖος = Nabataean, the ethnonym of the Nabataean people (the trading civilization centered in Petra). The Nabataean name (Semitic nbṭ) is a proper ethnic/place name. The Arabic root نبت may share the same Semitic root as the ethnic name, but this is an ethnonym — a proper name of a people. Excluded per proper-name rule.
key_insight: Ναβαταῖος is the proper ethnic name for the Nabataean people; comparing it to Arabic grow/sprout treats a proper name as a common noun.

semantic_journey: Arabic خظظ (khazza) / استرخى بطنه = the belly slackened/became loose; giving way/slackening of the belly. Greek χάζετο = he drew back, gave way, retreated (imperfect of χάζομαι). Both encode giving way/loosening. Arabic belly slackening/giving way → Greek physical retreating/giving way is a 1-step drift from bodily slackening to general giving way/retreat — the same yielding concept at body → action level.
key_insight: Arabic belly-slackening/yielding (khazz) and Greek retreating/giving-way (khazetai) share the core of yielding/slackening.

semantic_journey: Arabic خصص (khassasa) = to specify, assign specially, make particular to one person; to appropriate/reserve exclusively. Greek σχήσουσα = she who will have/hold (future participle of ἔχω 'to have/hold'). Both encode the concept of holding/possessing something specifically one's own. Arabic exclusive-assignment → Greek having/holding is a 1-step drift: to assign exclusively to oneself is to hold it.
key_insight: Arabic exclusive-assignment (khasas) and Greek having/holding (ekhein) share the concept of something being specifically one's own.

semantic_journey: Arabic الكون (al-kawwana/al-kawn) variant form = the folding; Arabic كون from كوى = to fold, wind, coil. Greek ἑλίκοιν = of spirals/coils. Both encode coiling/winding movement. Arabic folding/winding → Greek spiral/coil is a direct 1-step match of the geometric winding concept.
key_insight: Arabic folding/winding (kawn) and Greek spiral (helix) share the coiling geometric concept.

semantic_journey: Arabic برذون (birdhaun) = a packhorse or mule, heavy working horse. Greek βουρδών = mule, packhorse. The original reasoning acknowledges that 'Arabic borrowed from Greek (via Byzantine).' This is the REVERSE direction — Arabic borrowed from Greek βουρδών (itself possibly from Gaulish or Latin burdo). This is not an Arabic-to-Greek cognate; it is Greek-to-Arabic. The direction of borrowing is inverted. False positive due to wrong direction.
key_insight: Arabic birdhaun was borrowed FROM Greek bourdon (mule); the direction is Greek→Arabic, not Arabic→Greek.

semantic_journey: Arabic حيض (haydh) = menstrual blood flow; continued flowing of blood beyond normal period. Greek χυδαῖος = poured out copiously, flowing freely, also: common/vulgar (from χέω 'to pour'). Both encode the concept of copious flowing/pouring. Arabic continued copious blood flow → Greek copious outpouring/flowing is a direct 1-step match: both describe excessive flowing of liquid beyond its proper bounds.
key_insight: Arabic copious blood flow (haydh) and Greek copious outpouring (khudaios) share the concept of liquid flowing beyond normal bounds.

semantic_journey: Arabic الفوز (al-fawz) = success, attainment, winning; أفازه = he caused him to attain/gain/win. Greek εἰλήφατε = you have taken/received (perfect of λαμβάνω 'to take, receive, obtain'). Both encode the concept of obtaining/receiving/gaining something. Arabic causing-to-attain/win → Greek you-have-obtained is a direct 1-step match: attainment = receiving/taking possession of the goal.
key_insight: Arabic attainment/winning (fawz) and Greek having-taken/received (eilephate) share the core concept of obtaining something successfully.
